segments.s196-g145: ز is the preposition 'from/by' placed before its noun (poetic ordering). تیزی is the noun 'keenness/sharpness' (here meaning the sharp edge or swiftness that acts on someone). گرفتار شد is a light-verb clause 'became trapped/was caught' with شد as the past-form auxiliary 'became' placed after the participial adjective گرفتار. ریونیز appears at the end (inverted, poetic word order) as the patient/subject name being described as 'caught'.
segments.s196-g146: نبود is an existential negative 'there was not / it was not' (past); the sequence places the verb first for emphasis. از بد بختِ ما مانده چیز is a prepositional phrase + nominal phrase: از (from/of) + بدبختِ ما ('our bad fate' where بد qualifies بخت and ما is the possessor) + مانده چیز ('remaining thing', with مانده a past-participial/adjectival form). Together: 'there was no remaining thing of our bad fate' => 'nothing remained of our misfortune.'
segments.s196-g147: هنر بی‌خرد is a noun followed by a postpositive modifier: 'skill/virtué without wisdom' (بی‌خرد = 'unwise'). در دل مرد places that quality 'in the heart of a man' (در دل = 'in the heart/mind of'); مرد تند is a compact noun phrase 'an impetuous/quick-tempered man' where تند qualifies مرد. The order reflects classical/poetic Persian (modifier after the noun and prepositional phrase preceding the possessor).
segments.s196-g148: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. تیغی is an indefinite 'a sword' (here the comparison image). که opens a relative clause; گردد is a subjunctive/present-type verb 'becomes' or 'is turned' and ز marks the cause/agent 'by/from' with زنگار 'rust' as the cause. کند is the main verb 'makes/does' of that relative clause: the whole relative clause describes 'a sword which rust makes become (spoiled/dulled)'. The clause thus parallels the first half: an unwise skill in an impetuous man is like a sword that rust spoils.
segments.s196-g149: چو here functions temporal/concessive 'when/after'. چندین بگفتند literally 'they said several (things)' — چندین quantifies the speech and بگفتند is the past plural 'they said'. آب از دو چشم is the result clause 'water (i.e., tears) from both eyes' with از marking source and دو چشم 'both eyes'. Together: 'When they said several (things), tears fell from both eyes.'
segments.s196-g150: ببارید is the intransitive past 'fell' (drops/tears fell). و links clauses 'and'. آمد is 'came' (past). ز تندی sets the cause/source 'from the swiftness/fierceness' (تندی here = fierceness/vehemence). بخشم is actually the contracted/poetic form corresponding to به خشم 'into anger' or 'my anger' — grammatically it means 'my wrath/anger came' (i.e., 'I was filled with anger').
segments.s196-g151: چنین پاسخ آورد is a fixed expression 'he brought/returned such an answer' (چنین = 'thus', پاسخ = 'reply', آورد = 'brought/said'). کز is the contracted classical conjunction 'که از / که ز' meaning 'that/from which' introducing the clause explaining the reason or content. بخت بد is a noun phrase 'bad fate/ill fortune' serving as the topic/agent in the following subordinate clause.
segments.s196-g152: بسی here is classical/poetic intensifier 'very/much' (used adverbially) followed by the nouns رنج و سختی 'suffering and hardship'. بمردم رسد is a verbal phrase where رسد 'befalls/comes to' is the finite verb and بمردم (in context 'به مردم' = 'to people' / 'upon men') indicates the experiencer or target; together: 'much suffering and hardship befall people.' Note: بعضِ واژه‌ها در اینجا به شکل کلاسیک آمده‌اند و ترتیب کلمات برای وزن شعری جابجا شده است.
segments.s196-g169: دل سنگ is a nominal phrase meaning “stone heart” (دل + adjective/qualifier سنگ) where the modifier follows the head for poetic emphasis. سندان is another noun joined by و (‘and’) to make a compound subject. بترسد is a third-person singular present-form with the verbal prefix ب- (classical/poetic present/subjunctive/indicative usage) meaning “fears/should fear”; note that classical Persian often allows a singular verb with a coordinated subject for stylistic effect. ز is the poetic short form of از, a preposition meaning “from” or “because of,” linking مرگ as the object (“fear of death”).
segments.s196-g170: رهایی is a noun meaning “release/deliverance” placed before the verb as the patient/theme. نیابد is the negative present/optative third‑person singular of یافتن (with classical negation pattern نیابـ-), here meaning “does not find / will not obtain.” ازو is the fused poetic form of از + او (“from it/him”), referring back to مرگ (‘death’) in the previous segment. بار و برگ is a paired expression (literally “load and leaf”) used idiomatically to mean burdens/possessions; grammatically it is the object of نیابد.
segments.s196-g153: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (b- is the classical prefixed form). تا here functions as a subordinating conjunction 'that / so that' introducing the subordinate clause. دخمه (tomb) is the direct object and شاهوار follows as an adjectival/epithetic modifier with the possessive or descriptive sense 'lofty/kingly.' The phrase literally = 'he commanded that a lofty tomb...'.
segments.s196-g154: بکردند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they made/they caused' (b- prefixed form common in classical Persian). بر is the preposition 'on/upon.' تیغ here is used metaphorically as 'point/edge/summit' rather than literally 'sword.' آن is the demonstrative 'that' and کوهسار is 'mountain/ridge.' Word order is object/phrase then verb: 'they made (it) on the point of that mountain.'
segments.s196-g155: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'to place, to set' (here a verb 'they placed'). زیراندرش is a fused locative + pronoun ('زیر اندر' = 'underneath' with -ش 'it/him'), so 'beneath it/him.' تخت زر = 'throne of gold' or 'golden throne' (Persian often places the material/possessive after the noun).
segments.s196-g156: بدیبای here is given as a proper name/epithet (likely describing the cloth or cushion). زربفت is a compound adjective 'gold-brocaded' (زَر + بَفت). و is the coordinating 'and.' زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying کمر 'girdle/belt' (here 'golden girdle'). The whole segment lists ornamental items associated with the throne.
segments.s196-g157: تن is 'body.' شاهوارش is the adjective 'kingly/lofty' with the possessive suffix -ش 'his' attached to the adjective (so 'his kingly/lofty'). بیاراستند is the 3rd-person plural past of آراستن with the prefixed b- (classical style) meaning 'they adorned/arrayed.' The clause is subject understood = 'they adorned his kingly body.'
segments.s196-g158: This is a simple enumerative clause: گل 'flowers,' مشک 'musk,' کافور 'camphor' joined by و 'and.' می‌خواستند is the imperfect/past habitual 'they wished/they intended' (literally 'they were wanting'), indicating desire or intent continuing in the past and linking to the actions that follow.
segments.s196-g159: سرش = سر + -ش (his head). را is the definite-object marker, marking 'his head' as the object. بکافور is a prefixed instrumental phrase 'with camphor' (b- + کافور). کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' (here 'they anointed/applied'), and خشک ('dry') functions as a resultative adjective/adverb meaning 'dry' (they anointed/dried it with camphor). The sequence shows instrument (بکافور) placed before the verb in classical usage.
segments.s196-g160: رخش is the name/common noun for the steed (Rakhsh); را marks it as object. بعطر = 'with perfume' (b- prefixed instrumental), و 'and', گلاب 'rosewater', و, بمشک 'with musk' — a coordinated list of fragrances used to anoint/perfume the horse. Word order places the instrumentals (بعطر, بمشک) adjacent to the noun they affect.
segments.s196-g9: This is a prepositional/circumstantial list introduced by the poetic preposition ز (here 'from' / 'because of'), repeated for emphasis: ز گرد سواران و ز گرز و تیر = 'from the throng/round of horsemen and from maces and arrows.' گرد here means a gathered round/host (an older poetic usage before the noun), and the repeated ز links these causes to what follows.
segments.s196-g10: سر کوه is a compound meaning 'the summit/top of the mountain' (head of the mountain) and is the nominal subject; شد is the simple past 'became.' همچو introduces a simile 'like/as,' and دریای قیر is a genitive phrase 'a sea of tar.' The whole is a predication: 'the mountain-summits became like a sea of tar.'
segments.s196-g11: نبد is an archaic negative copular/existential 'was not / there was not' (so 'نبد هیچ هامون و جای نبرد' = 'there was no plain nor place of battle'). هیـچ negates 'هامون' (plain) and 'جای نبرد' is a noun phrase 'place of combat.' Word order places the verb first (classical/poetic style).
segments.s196-g12: همی here is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('even/indeed'). Grammatically کوه و سنگ is the nominal subject ('the mountains and rocks') and اسپ را is the definite object marked by را ('the steed'); خیره کرد is a causative/perfective construction 'caused to be astonished' (خیره = astonished/staring, کرد = made/caused). So: 'even the mountains and rocks caused the horse to be stunned/astonished.'
segments.s196-g13: ازین گونه is a fixed phrase meaning 'in this manner' (از + این/ین + گونه). تا is temporal 'until.' گشت is the past of گشتن 'turned / became' and خورشید راست literally 'the sun (to) the right' — an idiom for the sun's turning (i.e., the passage of the day); together the clause gives a temporal frame: 'until the sun turned to the right.'
segments.s196-g14: سپاه فرود is a genitive phrase 'the army of Farud' — here فرود functions as a proper name (despite the lemma senses listed). دلاور is an epithet 'the brave' in apposition. بکاست is an archaic/perfective past meaning 'was diminished / decreased' (بـ- prefix + کاست(ن)). The clause means 'Farud's host, the brave, was reduced.'
segments.s196-g15: فراز و نشیبش = 'its highs and lows' (فراز 'heights', نشیب 'low places') with the possessive -ش; همه + کشته شد is a passive/result: 'all were killed' or 'all became slain.' The verb شد here yields a perfective/passive sense: 'were slain.'
segments.s196-g16: سربخت here is used as a proper name (a person) despite the lemma; مرد جوان is an appositive 'the young man.' گشته شد is the compound perfect/passive (گشته + شد) giving 'was made/was become' used idiomatically to mean 'was slain / fell' in context. So: 'Sarbakht, the young man, was killed.'
segments.s196-g73: همی here is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'ever/indeed' placed before the verb; خورد is used in the sense 'befalls/comes to' rather than 'eat'. باید is the impersonal modal 'must/ought'. کسی را که هست is a relative phrase 'anyone who is (as he is)' with را marking the definite/affected person and هست as the copula 'is'.
segments.s196-g74: منم is emphatic 'I am' (the pronoun plus copula); تنگدل is a predicative adjective 'close‑hearted/melancholic'. تا here has a causal/resultive sense 'so/therefore', linking the state to the result that follows. شدم is the past of 'become' and تنگدست is the resulting adjective 'impoverished/poor'.
segments.s196-g75: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. خود is emphatic 'yourself'. نزادی is the (negative) perfective sense 'did (not) bear/produce' used here about begetting or producing (figuratively 'bringing forth') a person. خردمند مرد is a noun phrase 'a wise man' (خردمند modifies مرد).
segments.s196-g76: ندیدی is the past negative 'you have not seen' (experience/observation). ز گیتی is the poetic 'in/from the world' with ز meaning 'in/from'. چنین گرم و سرد is a compact adjective phrase 'so (so) hot and cold', describing extreme or changeable temperament; و simply coordinates گرم and سرد.
segments.s196-g77: بباید is the impersonal modal 'one must/it is necessary'. به introduces the state 'in/into' (entering a condition) so به کوری و ناکام زیست means 'to live in blindness and frustration/unsuccess'. زیست is the infinitive/past-stem 'to live' used as the verbal noun here; کوری and ناکام are nouns/adjectives describing that life.
segments.s196-g78: برین زندگانی is the prepositional phrase 'over/for this life' (بر + این written together as برین); بباید again gives the impersonal necessity 'one must' and گریست is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to weep' or an imperative-like lament 'one must weep'. Word order places the object/scene before the modal + verb for emphasis.
segments.s196-g79: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'at last/finally'. The verbless poetic clause 'خاکست بالین اوی' should be read as 'his pillow [was] ashes' — خاکست 'ashes/dust' as predicate and بالین اوی 'his pillow/bedside' with اوی as a possessive pronoun 'his'. The copula is omitted, as is common in verse.
segments.s196-g80: دریغ is an interjection/exclamation 'pity/alas'. آن points to 'that' (the person just mentioned). The coordinated nouns دل و رای و آیین list what is mourned: دل 'heart', رای 'counsel/opinion', and آیین 'custom/way', and اوی at the end is the possessive 'of his', applying to the whole list.
segments.s196-g1: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خورشید تابنده is a noun + adjective phrase ('the shining sun') and بنمود is a past-tense verb 'showed/appeared'; چهر (face) is the direct object of بنمود. Word order is Subject (خورشید تابنده) + Verb (بنمود) + Object (چهر), a normal Persian order in poetry.
segments.s196-g2: خرامان is an adverbial participle meaning 'with a stately/proud gait' modifying the verb برآمد ('arose/issued forth'), which is past tense. بخم سپهر is a poetic, somewhat archaic nominal phrase: بخم here is an old/obscure word forming a compound with سپهر ('the crest/summit of the sky/heavens'), and it functions as the subject that 'arose'.
segments.s196-g3: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side') placed frontally for emphasis. برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. خروش سران is a genitive phrase 'the uproar/cry of the chiefs' (خروش = uproar, سران = chiefs/leaders), functioning as the subject of برآمد.
segments.s196-g4: گراییدن is an infinitival/verbal-noun form 'the turning/swinging' that explains the cause or manner (it is linked to خروش: 'the uproar at the turning'). گرزهای گران is a possessive/qualified noun phrase 'the heavy maces' (گرزهای plural + گران 'heavy/weighty'), acting as the logical object/agent of گراییدن.
segments.s196-g5: This is a coordination: غو کوس is a noun phrase 'the tumult/clamor of the drum' (غو = tumult, کوس = war-drum). با ناله کرنای is an adjunct introduced by با ('with') meaning 'with the lament/moaning of trumpets' (ناله = lament, کرنای = trumpet). The whole phrase lists sounds that accompany the scene.
segments.s196-g6: دم نای is a compound 'the breath/blast of the ney (reed‑flute)' with دم (breath/blast) governing نای (ney). سرغین و هندی درای are coordinated modifiers following the instrument phrase: سرغین ('dung/filthy') and هندی درای (a poetical compound where هندی='Indian' and درای='possessing' or 'having'), functioning adjectivally to qualify the timbre or character of the sounds. The sequence lists different qualities/kinds of sound.
segments.s196-g7: برون آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'came out/forth' (برون = out/forth, آمد = came) in past tense. از باره دژ فرود is a prepositional/nominal phrase giving origin or place: از 'from' + باره (an old poetic noun, here 'occasion/occasioned action' or 'assault/charge') + دژ ('fortress') + فرود ('descent/alighting'). Together they indicate that the action originated at the fortress (poetic, condensed word-order).
segments.s196-g8: دلیران ترکان is a noun phrase 'the brave Turks' (دلیران = brave ones, ترکان = Turks). هرآنکس که بود is a parenthetical relative clause 'whoever he/they were' (هرآنکس = whoever, که = relative particle 'who/that', بود = past tense 'was/were'), attached to specify that the identity of those brave Turks may be any or unspecified.
segments.s196-g161: نهادند is the 3rd person plural past of نهادن 'to place' (they placed); the direct object is omitted but understood from context. بر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'on the throne' following the verb. و گشتند باز is a coordinated clause: گشتند is 3rd pl. past of گشتن 'they turned/returned' and باز is the adverb 'back/again' placed after the verb for poetic word order (more common prose order would be باز گشتند).
segments.s196-g162: شد is the 3rd person singular past of شدن 'to become/occur' here used to introduce the resulting state ('rose/was made'); the inversion شد آن ... places the verb before the noun for emphasis. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'; شیردل is a compound adjective/noun 'lion‑hearted' describing the شاه 'king', and گردن‌فراز is a postpositive adjective 'proud/lofty' that further characterizes him.
segments.s196-g163: زراسپ is a proper name (the person placed); سرافراز is an epithet 'honored/lofty' attached to the name. با introduces a comitative phrase 'with' and is followed by the companion's name ریونیز. The sequence puts the epithet immediately after the name (poetic flexibility).
segments.s196-g164: نهادند again is 'they placed' (3rd pl. past). در پهلوی شاه is the locative prepositional phrase 'at the king's side' (در + پهلوی 'side/flank' + شاه as the possessor). نیز at the end means 'also/too', marking that this placement is in addition to whatever was already done.
segments.s196-g165: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a general) serving as the head noun. بران is an old/poetic linking particle here meaning roughly 'with/that' and connects the head noun to the following descriptive phrase. ریش کافورگون is a noun + adjective 'beard camphor‑colored' that characterizes the spahbed; the adjective follows the noun as is common in Persian.
segments.s196-g166: ببارید is the 3rd person singular past of باریدن 'to fall/rain' used metaphorically for a flow. از دیدگان is the prepositional phrase 'from the eyes' (از + دیدگان 'eyes'), and جوی خون 'a stream of blood' is the flowing substance; poetic word order places the verb before the phrases it relates to.
segments.s196-g167: چنینست is a fixed copular phrase 'thus it is' or 'such is the case'. هرچند is a concessive conjunction 'although/however' introducing the subordinate idea. مانیم is 1st person plural present/subjunctive form of ماندن 'we may remain/we remain' used here in a generalizing sense, and دیر is an adverb 'long (of time)' describing duration.
segments.s196-g168: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. پیل سرافراز ماند is 'the proud elephant remained/lasted' with ماند as 3rd sing. past of ماندن 'to remain/endure' (here used as general statement), and شیر is the second term 'the lion' in the negation.
segments.s196-g89: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' placed first (poetic inversion). ببالین is a locative compound meaning 'to/at the bedside'. فرخ فرود is the personal name of the one who came and is placed after the verb and locative by poetic word order.
segments.s196-g90: یکی introduces an indefinite single item ('one, a certain'); دشنه is the noun 'dagger'. با او here marks accompaniment/possession 'with him/it'. چو + noun phrase ('چو آب کبود') is a simile 'like dark-blue water' modifying the dagger's appearance.
segments.s196-g91: دو رخ را is a definite/accusative object ('the two faces' with را marking the object). بروی پسر is the locative phrase 'on the (young) man's face' (بروی = بر روی, a contracted/prepositional phrase + following noun). نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'placed/put' completing the verb phrase; overall word order is object + locative + verb due to poetic inversion.
segments.s196-g92: شکم is the noun 'stomach/belly' and بردرید is the past tense 'they tore/cut open' acting on that object. و simply links clauses 'and'. برش here is a nominal/verb cluster meaning roughly 'and (to) him' or 'and then' in context (poetic contraction), جان is 'life', and بداد is an old past form meaning 'gave' — together expressing 'and (thereupon) he gave up his life.'
segments.s196-g93: در دژ is a locative 'in the fortress'. بکندند is a past plural verb 'they (broke in / dug / forced)'; ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians' placed after the verb (poetic inversion: verb before subject). The clause describes the Iranians' action inside the citadel.
segments.s196-g94: بغارت is a noun here (likely 'the booty/plunder' or a proper/obscure name in manuscripts) serving as the object. ببستند is the past plural 'they closed/tied up/secured'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether', and میان is a locative 'in the middle / among'. The sequence gives the result of the Iranians' action: they secured the booty wholly in the interior.
segments.s196-g95: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بهرام is the subject (proper name). نزدیک means 'near/approaching' and آن باره 'that occasion/place/incident' forms the adverbial phrase; شد is the past auxiliary 'became / was' completing the clause 'when Bahram approached that place.'
segments.s196-g96: از indicates cause/agent here ('because of/from'); اندوه is 'sorrow'. یکسر is an adverb 'completely/entirely'. دلش = دل + ش 'his heart'. پاره شد is a compound meaning 'was torn' (پاره 'torn' + شد 'became/was'), so the clause means 'his heart was entirely torn from sorrow.'
segments.s196-g81: This clause is a compact past action: subject (پرستندگان) + prepositional phrase (بر سر دژ) + past verb شدند. In classical Persian the verb شدن here reports the result/arrival ('ended up / went / were') rather than a literal 'become'; word order is subject → circumstantial phrase → verb.
segments.s196-g82: همه خویشتن is an emphatic plural subject phrase ('all themselves' = 'they all'); بر زمین is the prepositional complement ('onto the ground'); برزدند is a past transitive verb used here to mean a forcible action of casting/striking down (poetic usage for 'threw themselves down/prostrated'). The order remains subject → adjunct → verb.
segments.s196-g83: یکی here functions as 'one (person)' (indefinite subject). آتشی خود جریره is a genitive/group: آتشی (fiery / a fire) qualifying جریره (a small place/islet) with خود marking possession ('his own'); فروخت is an archaic/poetic usage meaning 'kindled' or 'set alight' rather than 'sold'.
segments.s196-g84: همه گنجها را is the definite object marked by را ('all the treasures'); به آتش is the goal/means ('into the fire'); بسوخت is the perfective verb meaning 'burned up' (active/declarative past). The clause reads literally 'he burned all the treasures into the fire.'
segments.s196-g85: یکی begins a new actor ('another, one'); تیغ is the direct object and بگرفت is the past tense verb 'seized/took up.' زان پس is a temporal connector ('after that/thereupon'), and بدست is an adverbial phrase 'in (his) hand' describing how he held the sword — the second clause implies the continued action of holding after seizing.
segments.s196-g86: در خانه is the locative phrase ('inside the house'); تازی اسپان is a genitive/attributive cluster where تازی ('Arabian') qualifies اسپان ('horses'). ببست is the past verb 'he bound/tied' (transitive), so the whole clause means he tied up the Arabian horses in the house.
segments.s196-g87: شکمشان is the possessed noun 'their bellies'; بدرید is the past of دریدن 'they tore/cut open.' The coordinating و links to ببرید پی where ببرید is a past verb (here a poetic form of بریدن 'they cut') and پی ('one after another/after') indicates successive or continuing action. The clause packs two violent past actions in sequence.
segments.s196-g88: همی is a classical continuous/presentive prefix/adverb marking ongoing action in narrative past ('was / kept on'); ریخت is the verb 'poured/flowed'; از دیده is the source phrase 'from the eyes'; خوناب و خوی are the parallel nouns listing what poured out (bloody discharge / blood-gore). The word order is adverb → verb → source → objects.
segments.s196-g49: چنین (thus) opens the speech; گفت is the main past-tense verb 'said' (the narrator's reporting verb). چون introduces a temporal clause 'when/as' (classical usage), followed by the action لب ز هم برگرفت where the verb برگرفت (lit. 'took away') is perfective/past and carries the sense 'withdrew' or 'drew apart'. Word order is Subject-Verb omitted here (understood subject = he); ز marks 'from' and هم is an emphatic/together particle attached to لب ('lips') giving the reciprocal/collective sense 'from one another' or simply 'apart'.
segments.s196-g50: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. این موی کندن is an infinitival noun phrase 'this hair‑plucking' (این + موی + verbal noun کندن). نباشد is an archaic/poetic negative copula form (here functioning like 'is not' / 'would not be' in reported speech), and شگفت is a noun 'a wonder' — so the clause literally says 'that this hair‑plucking is not a wonder.'
segments.s196-g51: کنون is an adverb 'now'; اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'in/within'; آیند is a present/future-oriented verb form 'they come/they will come' (third person plural); ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians'. Classical Persian often places adverb + locative before subject + verb.
segments.s196-g52: به تاراج is a purposive phrase 'to plunder' (به + noun 'tāraj' functioning like an infinitival purpose). دژ پاک بسته میان is a string of modifiers: دژ 'fortress' + پاک 'pure/clean' (epithet) + بسته 'shut/closed' (adjectival/participle) + میان 'in the middle' or 'amidst'. The phrase describes the object/place toward which the Iranians come to pillage.
segments.s196-g53: پرستندگان را is a definite/direct-object phrase 'the worshippers' (را marks the definite object). اسیران کنند uses اسیران as a predicative/object complement 'prisoners' and کنند (third person plural of کردن) as a causative/performative 'make' or simply 'do' — together meaning 'they will take the worshippers captive' (literally 'they make the worshippers [to be] prisoners').
segments.s196-g54: This coordinate clause lists further actions: دژ (the fortress) followed by وباره 'and again' (repetitive/adverbial), then کوه ویران کنند where کوه is 'mountain' and ویران (here verbal/adjectival) with کنند gives 'they destroy/lay waste'. Word order is object(s) then verb (کنند) as is normal in Persian.
segments.s196-g55: دل هرک بر من بسوزد همی is an optative/wish construction: دل هرک 'the heart of whoever' + بر من 'for me' + بسوزد (third person singular subjunctive/jussive of سوزیدن) 'may ... burn/ache'. همی is a classical particle adding intensity or continuous aspect ('ever/indeed'). The structure expresses a fervent wish or curse: 'may whoever's heart burns for me be consumed.'
segments.s196-g56: ز جانم is a prepositional phrase 'from my life' or 'with respect to my life' (ز = 'from'); رخش is here 'his beauty/countenance' rather than the steed sense, and برفروزد همی is another optative/subjunctive 'may ... kindle/ignite' with همی again intensifying. Together the clause reads as a wish: 'may his beauty kindle (set) my life ablaze.' Note the detached possessive-like element م in the data is actually the enclitic '‑م' ('my') attached to جان (here shown separately).
segments.s196-g137: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so'. گفت is the past tense of گفتن ('said') here; the order is adverb + verb + subject, so گودرز (the subject) follows گفت. با + proper names (طوس و گیو) marks accompaniment ('with Tous and Giv').
segments.s196-g138: همان is a demonstrative meaning 'the same/that very'. نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. و links the two nouns; گردان is a troop/band (here a noun). نیو is left as a name/epithet in apposition to گردان (sense unclear in the supplied options).
segments.s196-g139: که introduces a clause meaning 'that' or 'for'. تندی 'haste' is the subject; نه before کار + predicate negates the claim (here 'was not the work of a spahbed'). سپهبد بود is the predicate 'was (the) spahbed's affair/task'.
segments.s196-g140: This is a conditional/characterizing clause: 'سپهبد که تندی کند' = 'a spahbed who makes/does haste' (که as a relative/introducing particle). کند is the present/habitual verb 'does/acts' used to state a general truth; بد بود = 'is/was bad' (judgement).
segments.s196-g141: جوانی is a noun 'a youth' or 'youthfulness'. بدین سان is a fixed phrase 'in this manner' (here split in the word-list into parts); ز is the poetic preposition 'from', تخم 'seed/stock' and کیان 'the Kians/the royal house' forms a genitive 'from the seed of the Kians'.
segments.s196-g142: بدین فر = 'with this فر' where بدین (literally 'in/with this') qualifies فر ('royal glory'); و links the following qualities. این برز = 'this/that exaltation or stature'; یال و میان are literal nouns 'mane and middle' used here as epithets of strength/appearance. The sequence lists attributes of that youth.
segments.s196-g143: بدادی is the past form 'you (or he) gave' (the supplied lemma senses don't match the verbal use). The two nouns بتیزی and تندی form the direct objects (qualities bestowed). و links them; بباد is a passive/resultive expression 'be blown away / be swept away' describing the outcome.
segments.s196-g144: زرسپ is a proper name (Zarasp). آن is a demonstrative 'that' used emphatically before the title سپهدار ('commander'); نوذرنژاد is an attributive family/lineage name 'of Nowzar's birth/stock' in apposition, identifying the commander.
segments.s196-g129: سیاوش is the proper name (subject). بد خفته is a compounded adjectival/participial phrase: خفته is the past participle 'asleep/lying' and بد 'ill/badly' qualifies it, giving the sense 'ill‑asleep' or 'lying in a bad state'. بر تخت زر is a locative prepositional phrase 'on the throne of gold' (تخت 'throne' + زر 'gold' in postnominal position, a common Persian genitive/epithet order). The clause is verbless here because the main action is carried by the next segment.
segments.s196-g130: ابا is used here as a verb/verb‑like predicate meaning 'to refuse / be reluctant (to take or wear)'; it governs the following list of objects. جوشن و تیغ و گرز و کمر is a coordinated series of direct objects ('cuirass/armor, sword, mace, and belt/girdle'), joined by و. The verb appears with its objects in a compact poetic sequence; the action is that he refused these items.
segments.s196-g131: برو زار functions here as an adverbial/qualifying phrase meaning 'in deep distress / woefully' (a poetic collocation of برو + زار). بگریست is a past tense verb 'wept/wailed' placed before its subjects (verb‑first order is common in poetry). The following nouns گودرز و گیو are the subjects 'Goudarz and Giv' (the verb precedes the compound subject).
segments.s196-g132: بزرگان is the nominative plural 'the great ones / chiefs'. چو is the comparative particle 'like / as', introducing an example. گرگین و بهرام نیو are names given as examples (Gorgin and Bahram Niu); و simply joins them. In this short clause چو links the chiefs to those named, i.e. 'the chiefs — such as Gorgin and Bahram Niu.'
segments.s196-g133: رخ طوس is a construct 'the face/countenance of Tūs'. شد is the past verb 'became'. پر ز introduces a measure/complement 'full of' (پر used adjectivally after شد). خون جگر is a poetic compound 'the blood of the jigar (heart/liver)', where جگر is used figuratively as 'heart/seat of feeling' — together meaning something like 'blood of the heart' or 'heart‑blood', a metaphor for severe bleeding or deep anguish.
segments.s196-g134: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. درد here carries the sense of inner anguish/sorrow. فرود is used as a noun/participial notion 'descent / fall' (i.e. 'he fell / sank down') and is to be read with the implied subject from the previous clause (Tūs). The clause is elliptical: 'and ز درد پسر' extends the same idea to 'and (so) the son (from pain)' — the verb is omitted by ellipsis and understood: the son likewise sank/was afflicted by pain.
segments.s196-g135: که is the conjunction 'that / for', introducing the clause. تندی is the subject 'haste' (abstract noun). پشیمانی is the object 'regret/repentance'. آردت is the verb آرد 'brings' with the enclitic -ت 'to you' (i.e. 'will bring you' or 'brings you'); بار is a noun 'load/burden' functioning as the resultative complement 'a burden (of regret)'. The clause thus means 'that haste will bring you a burden of regret.'
segments.s196-g136: تو is the emphatic pronoun 'you'. در بوستان is a locative phrase 'in the garden' (در + بوستان). تخم is 'seed' and is the head of the noun phrase; تندی مکار is a double postpositive modification: تندی 'haste' and مکار 'deceitful / crafty'—together 'the seed of deceitful haste' (Persian places modifiers after the head noun). The line addresses the addressee: 'you have planted (in the garden) the seed of deceptive haste.'
segments.s196-g41: بشد is an archaic/historical narrative verb meaning 'it happened' / 'he appeared' (here: 'he came/appeared'). It is followed by a prepositional phrase با + NP (با پرستندگان مادرش) meaning 'with his mother's devotees' (مادرش = mother + possessive clitic ش). Word order is Verb + prepositional accompaniment.
segments.s196-g42: گرفتند is simple past, 3rd‑person plural 'they seized/took'. پوشیدگان is a nominalized participle 'the covered/veiled ones' (object). در is the preposition 'into / in', and برش here functions as a pronominal or locative phrase ('on/around/to her') in classical orthography (بر + ش). The clause means 'they seized the veiled ones and put/took them to her.'
segments.s196-g43: بزاری here functions as a noun 'wailing/lament' (not merely an adjective). فگندند is past plural 'they threw/they cast'. بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'upon the ivory throne' (تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). The clause reads 'they cast (their) wailing upon the ivory throne.'
segments.s196-g44: نبد is an archaic negative/existential verb meaning 'was not / did not exist' (used before the noun). شاه را uses را as the definite/object/topic marker ('the king'). روز هنگام + تاج is a temporal phrase 'at that time of day (with regard to) the crown.' The whole clause: 'the king's crown was not (upon him) at that time of day.'
segments.s196-g45: همه is 'all/every'. غالیه موی is a poetic phrase meaning 'precious locks of hair' (غالیه as 'precious/valuable thing' qualifying موی 'hair'). مشکلن کمند (مشکین کمند) is a compound describing a tress — مشکین here = 'dark/black' (poetic color) and کمند used metaphorically for a 'curl/tress' (a literal 'lasso' sense is secondary). The phrase lists the things that will be pulled.
segments.s196-g46: This clause names the agents and the action: a compound subject (پرستنده و مادر = 'the worshipper(s) and the mother') + از بن ('from the root') + بکند, an archaic/historic present form (ب‑کند) used in epic narrative to express the action 'pull out / pluck out' here. Word order is Subject + prepositional phrase + verb; context supplies the object (the locks named before).
segments.s196-g47: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually / repeatedly' in classical Persian and often precedes the verb. کند here is the classical present/habitual form (used in narrative) 'does / kept (doing)'. جان (life) is the direct object and آن گرامی ('that honored one') is the referent; فرود (literally 'descent') describes the falling/decline of the life (i.e., dying). The clause means 'that honored man's life kept falling away.'
segments.s196-g48: This is a paired, parallel description: همه تخت مویه = 'the whole throne (was) lamentation' (تخت = throne; مویه = lament) and همه حصن رود = 'the whole fortress (was) a river' (حصن = fortress; رود = river). The verbs are omitted (elliptical) and the nouns are linked by parallel structure to paint a metaphorical scene of widespread weeping.
segments.s196-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. از وی جدا گشت is a verb phrase 'became separated from him' (گشت here = 'became/turned'), with the items that separated (بازوی و دوش) placed after the verb in poetic word order. بازوی و دوش are the things that have become separated (subject/affected parts).
segments.s196-g34: همی is the classical progressive particle marking continuous action before past verbs. تاخت اسپ is a V + noun sequence 'galloped (the) steed' with verb before noun (poetic order). همی زد خروش = 'and (it) uttered a cry', where زد takes خروش as its object ('to sound/utter' here).
segments.s196-g35: بنزدیک دژ is an adverbial phrase 'near the fortress' (بنزدیک = 'near'), followed by the name بیژن as subject and اندر رسید 'arrived/entered in' as the verbal predicate. اندر marks location 'into/within'.
segments.s196-g36: بزخمی is an adjectival/participial modifier 'wounded'. پی باره here expresses recurrence/sequence ('again; once more') and او is the object pronoun 'him'. برید is the past action 'cut/struck' (classical بریدن) — a poetic past verb meaning 'he struck/attacked'.
segments.s196-g37: پیاده خود = 'he himself on foot' (پیاده = 'on foot'; خود emphatic 'himself'). و چند زان چاکران = 'and several of those servants' with زان (= 'of/from that') pointing back to the previously mentioned servants; word order reflects poetic terseness.
segments.s196-g38: تبه گشته is a perfect participle meaning 'have been ruined/perished' (تبه here = 'ruin/mischief' used in sense 'ruined'). از + noun indicates source: از چنگ کنداوران = 'from the clutches/grip of the Kandavars' (چنگ = 'claw/grip'). کنداوران is a group name here (enemy), treated as a source; it is an archaic/poetic ethnonym.
segments.s196-g39: بدژ در شد is a fused prepositional + noun + verb meaning 'entered into the fortress' (بدژ = به دژ, 'to the fortress'); در شد = 'entered (the gate)' with در = 'door/gate' and شد = 'became/entered' (poetic past). و در ببستند = 'and they shut/closed the gate' (ببستند = 3rd plural past); زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon'.
segments.s196-g40: شد here is a narrative past marker 'there came/appeared'. آن نامور شیر جنگی فرود is a nominal phrase 'that famed, warlike lion', with فرود ('descent/alighting') qualifying the lion—i.e. 'the lion that alighted/descended'. Word order places the verb-like شد before the descriptive nominal phrase (classical/poetic order).
segments.s196-g25: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past-tense circumstantial clause; the verb phrase پدید آمد (literally ‘appeared/came forth’) is the verbal core and follows the subject بیژن as is common in Persian narrative order. اندر نشیب is a prepositional phrase 'in/onto the descent/slope' (اندر = 'in/within' + نشیب = 'descent'), giving the location for the appearance.
segments.s196-g26: This is two coordinated predications linked by و. Each uses the copular/lexical شد 'became' with an adjective before the verb: سبک شد عنان = 'the reins became light' and گران شد رکیب = 'the rider/companion became heavy'. In poetic word order the adjective+شد can precede the noun it predicates; عنان and رکیب are the nouns undergoing that change.
segments.s196-g27: This clause strings nominal/participial elements before the verb: فرود (alighting/descent) with جوان ('the young man') sets the scene; ترگ بیژن is a genitival phrase 'Bijan's rag' (ترگ = rag), and the finite verb بدید 'saw' (past) closes the clause. In other words: 'The young man who alighted saw Bijan's rag.'
segments.s196-g28: Two coordinated actions follow: بزد دست is a short past-clause 'he struck (with) [his] hand' where بزد is the verb 'he hit/struck' and دست is the instrument/part referenced. The second conjunct تیغ از میان برکشید combines تیغ 'sword' with the light verb برکشید 'drew/raised' and the prepositional phrase از میان 'from between/from among' — i.e. 'he drew a sword from between (his garments/sides)'.
segments.s196-g29: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. رهام is the proper name (Roham) and گرد اندر آمد 'came around/rounded' is a phrasal motion (گرد = around/about, اندر آمد = 'came into/within'), followed by به پشت 'to the rear/behind' indicating location: 'When Roham came round to the rear...'.
segments.s196-g30: This is a descriptive verbless/nominal phrase modifying the subject: خروشان 'roaring' (present participial adjective) followed by یکی تیغ هندی 'one / a single Indian sword' (یکی = 'one / a certain', تیغ = 'sword', هندی = 'Indian') and the locative/instrumental phrase به مشت 'in the fist' (به + مشت) specifying how it was held. Overall: 'roaring, he held an Indian sword in his fist.'
segments.s196-g31: بزد is the past-tense verb 'he struck'. It takes the prepositional phrase بر سر کتف 'upon the head/shoulder' (بر = 'on/upon', سر = 'head/top' used here with کتف = 'shoulder') as its target, and مرد دلیر 'the brave man' is the noun phrase being described: 'he struck upon the shoulder of the brave man.' Word order places the verb first (typical in epic lines) with its prepositional object following.
segments.s196-g32: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'fell/descended' or 'alighted/fell upon' in the past. The phrase از دوش دستش به زیر uses از دوش ('from the shoulder' — دوش = 'shoulder') plus the enclitic possessive ش on دست meaning 'his hand' and به زیر 'down/beneath' to describe motion/result: 'his hand came down from the shoulder' (i.e. his hand dropped down beneath the shoulder).
segments.s196-g97: بایرانیان گفت کز... is a reporting clause: بایرانیان is the subject ('the Bayrans/the Iranians') and گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing reported speech. کز is the archaic subordinating particle 'that/from which' (here 'that'), and از پدر is the prepositional phrase ('because of / on account of his father') which links to the predicate in the reported clause.
segments.s196-g98: This is the reported-content clause. بسی functions as an intensifying adverb ('very/much'); خوارتر is the comparative adjective 'more humiliated/despised'; مرد here is the past tense of 'to die' (literal form looks identical to the noun but is used as a verb 'died'); و هم zarf/connector 'and also'; زارتر is another comparative adjective 'more wretched'. The sequence gives two comparative predicates modifying the same referent in the reported speech.
segments.s196-g99: کشنده سیاوش is a genitive N+N construction meaning 'the killer of Siyavash'; چاکر نبود is a verbless-predicate turned full clause where چاکر 'retainer/attendant' is the predicate and نبود is the past negated copula 'was not' — together 'had no attendant' or 'there was no attendant.'
segments.s196-g100: ببالینش is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('at his bedside/at his side'); بر here is a preposition 'on/over' used with کشته as an adjective/noun 'the slain (one)'; مادر is the substantive 'mother' and نبود is the past negative 'was not' — together: 'the slain man's mother was not at his side.' Word order is topical: location first (ببالینش), then the prepositional phrase modifying who was absent.
segments.s196-g101: همه as a determiner 'the whole/entire' modifies دژ 'fortress'; سراسر is an adverb meaning 'throughout/entirely' and bرافروخته is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'kindled/ablaze'. The clause is verbless in appearance but functions as 'the whole fortress was entirely aflame.'
segments.s196-g102: همه again 'all/every' quantifies the following compound خان و مان (a fixed collocation meaning houses/households/inns). کنده و سوخته are two past-participial adjectives joined by و describing state ('uprooted/demolished and burned'); the sequence gives a piled-up description of destruction.
segments.s196-g103: As before, بایرانیان گفت introduces reported speech. کز is the archaic subordinating particle here (='that' / 'from which') linking the report to its object; کردگار is the object of the verb in the reported clause (the Creator/God). The clause prepares the command/advice that follows.
segments.s196-g104: بترسید is the 2nd-person plural present-subjunctive/imperative form of 'to fear' used inside reported speech as a hortative/imperative ('fear! / beware of'). وز is a poetic contraction (literally و + از) introducing a second object to fear ('and from/also'); گردش روزگار is a noun phrase 'the turning / revolutions of time' i.e. 'the vicissitudes of fate.'
segments.s196-g17: بدو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'unto him / at him' placed before the verb for focus; خیره ماندند is a two-word predication meaning 'stood transfixed / stared' with ماندند in the simple past, third-person plural (they remained/stood). ایرانیان is the subject but is postposed after the verb for emphasis (a common poetic word order).
segments.s196-g18: که here functions causally ('for/because'); چون introduces a comparison ('as / like'); او is the third-person pronoun 'he'; ندیدند is past tense, third-person plural negative 'they did not see'; شیر ژیان is an idiomatic/compound epithet 'a lion-like man' (شیر = 'lion', ژیان used here as part of the epithet). Note the verb precedes the noun phrase (poetic order) and the whole clause gives the reason for their staring.
segments.s196-g19: ز here is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from' (meaning 'of the Turks'); ترکان is the noun 'the Turks'; نماند is past tense 'remained' used negatively with ایچ ('not at all / none') to mean 'none remained'; با او سوار literally 'a rider with him' — با is 'with' and سوار is 'rider/horseman'. The phrase says that not one Turk or mounted man remained alongside him.
segments.s196-g20: ندید is past 'did not see'; ایچ is the emphatic negative 'not at all / none'; تنها 'only / single' modifies رخ 'face' (here 'a single face / warrior's face'); کارزار is 'battle/field'. Word order is compact and poetic: 'they did not see, not a single face of the battlefield.'
segments.s196-g21: عنان را is the direct object with the definite marker را ('the reins'); بپیچید is a past action 'he turned / twisted' (پیچیدن) — here third-person singular past; و links the two actions; تنها برفت combines تنها 'alone' with برفت 'he went away / rode off' (archaic/poetic past form). Together: 'He turned the reins and went off alone.'
segments.s196-g22: ز بالا سوی دژ is a small adverbial phrase 'from above toward the fortress' (ز = 'from', بالا = 'high/above', سوی = 'toward', دژ = 'fortress'); خرامید is past 'he strode / swaggered' (خرامیدن) describing proud movement; تفت here appears after the verb and likely is a name or fixed epithet — its dictionary senses given do not match this usage (so sense marked -1). The line describes him striding down from the heights toward the citadel.
segments.s196-g23: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; رهام و بیژن are the coordinated subjects 'Roham and Bijan'; کمین is the noun 'ambush'; ساختند is the simple past, third-person plural 'they made / they set up'. Together: 'When Roham and Bijan made an ambush…'.
segments.s196-g24: فراز و نشیبش 'his heights and slopes' (فراز = high places/top, نشیبش = its descent/slopes — the possessive -ش refers back to the place/person involved); همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'continually / indeed'; تاختند is past plural 'they charged/rode swiftly' (تاختن). The phrase says they charged up and down over the rise and fall (attacking from all directions).
segments.s196-g65: ببازیگری is an adverbial/gerundial phrase (بـ + بازیگری) meaning 'in play' or 'as play'; ماند is the past/simple perfect verb 'remained'; the subject این چرخ مست ('this drunken Wheel') follows the verb by poetic inversion, with مست (an adjective) postposed to the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s196-g66: که introduces a relative clause modifying چرخ; بازی here means 'play/trick'; برآرد is a classical 3rd-singular verb form meaning 'brings forth/causes' (used here with a general/habitual sense); به هفتاد دست is an adverbial phrase 'into/through seventy hands' with به = 'into'.
segments.s196-g67: Each زمانی is an adverb meaning 'at times/at one time'; the following بخنجر and بتیغ use the prefixed بـ (short for با) as an instrumental 'with' ('with a dagger', 'with a sword'). The verb is omitted by ellipsis — the line lists alternations of means/instruments.
segments.s196-g68: Same pattern of alternating adverbials: زمانی introduces 'at times'; بباد here means 'be blown away' (passive/be carried off by the wind); و is the coordinating 'and'; بمیغ is instrumental 'by/with the blade'. Again the main verb is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s196-g69: زمانی functions as 'at times'; بدست (بـ + دست) here means 'by/at the hand of' (agency or source); یکی is indefinite 'someone/a certain one'; ناسزا is the noun 'an insult' — the clause is elliptical: a verb like 'was given' or 'received' is omitted.
segments.s196-g70: Again زمانی = 'at times'; خود is reflexive/emphatic 'himself'; از introduces the source 'from' (از درد و سختی = 'from pain and hardship'); رها is a predicative adjective/participle 'free' (the copula is omitted), so the phrase means 'he himself is freed from pain and hardship.'
segments.s196-g71: زمانی = 'sometimes'; دهد is the 3rd-person present/future classical form of 'to give' (here general/habitual); تخت، گنج و کلاه are a coordinated list of direct objects ('throne, treasure and a hat/cap'). The verb precedes its objects, which is common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s196-g72: The second زمانی phrase continues by contrast; the verb is omitted but understood from the previous clause (دهد). The nouns غم، رنج، خواری و چاه form a list of misfortunes ('sorrow, suffering, humiliation and the pit/trap'), with چاه used figuratively as a doom/trap.
segments.s196-g57: همه پاک بر باره باید شدن is an adverbial + modal construction: همه پاک (literally 'altogether/entirely') modifies the following phrase, بر باره 'on the occasion/at every time' is a prepositional circumstantial, and باید شدن combines the modal باید ('must') with شدن ('to become/occur') to express an impersonal necessity ('it must be that...' / 'one must ...'). Word order places the degree/adverbial before the circumstance and the modal.
segments.s196-g58: تن خویش را بر زمین بر زدن is the goal/infinitival complement of the previous modal: تن خویش را ('one's own body' with خوی + ش forming the possessive 'خویش' and را marking it as definite/direct object) is followed by the compound verbal phrase بر زدن ('to strike/throw upon' = بر + زدن) with بر زمین 'onto the ground' as its adverbial place. The structure is [object را] + [prepositional place] + [verb infinitive], typical for Persian infinitival complements.
segments.s196-g59: کجا بهر بیژن نماند یکی is a rhetorical question: کجا 'where' introduces the location, بهر بیژن 'for Bijan' is a beneficiary phrase placed after it, and نماند یکی literally 'one does not remain' (نماند = 'will remain/be left'); the indefinite یکی ('someone') is the logical subject. The inversion (question word + beneficiary + verb + indefinite) is typical for rhetorical questions in poetry.
segments.s196-g60: نمانم من ایدر مگر اندکی is a first‑person statement tied to the previous question: نمانم 'I will not remain' (first‑person present/future sense), with the explicit pronoun من (emphatic) and location ایدر 'here'. مگر introduces an exception 'except', followed by the quantified exception اندکی 'a little'. The negative verb + مگر pattern yields 'I shall not remain here except a little.'
segments.s196-g61: کشنده تن و جان من درد اوست places the nominal phrase 'کشنده تن و جان من' ('the killer of my body and soul') before the copular clause 'درد اوست' ('it is his pain'). Grammatically, درد اوست is the main copula ('his pain is'), and 'کشنده' functions as a nominal complement describing what that pain is (i.e., the killer of my body and soul). Note the possessive من attaches to جان to give 'my soul'.
segments.s196-g62: پرستار و گنجم چه در خورد اوست is an exclamatory/interrogative clause asking about value or relation: پرستار و گنجم lists two items ('nurse/caretaker and (my) treasure' in context), چه 'what' questions their relevance, and در خورد اوست is an idiomatic phrase meaning roughly 'what do they avail him / what do they matter to him.' The verb/copula ست closes the clause, so the whole line asks 'what are my nurse and my treasure to him?'
segments.s196-g63: بگفت این و رخسارگان کرد زرد is a compound past action: بگفت این 'he said this' (ب‑ prefix marks past), then و connects the next clause. رخسارگان (poetic plural 'faces/cheeks') is the object of کرد ('made/caused'), and زرد is an adjectival result ('pale'); together 'he made his cheeks pale' or 'his cheeks grew pale.'
segments.s196-g64: برآمد روانش بتیمار و درد has برآمد 'rose/issued forth' as the verb and روانش ('his soul/spirit', with the enclitic ش) as its subject; the following بتیمار و درد are predicative/adjunct states — 'sick/afflicted and in pain'. بتیمار is an archaic/compound form (ب‑ + تیمار) indicating a state of illness or being tended, so the clause reads 'his spirit rose up sick and in pain.'
segments.s196-g121: چو آمد ببالین آن کشته زار — چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when (it) came' (چو = 'when'; آمد = past), introducing the circumstance. ببالین = 'to/at the bedside' follows آمد and marks the place; آن کشته زار is the noun phrase 'that wretched/slain one' (آن = 'that'; کشته = 'slain'; زار = 'wretched, pitiable') and functions as the subject/thing brought.
segments.s196-g122: بران تخت با مادر افگنده خوار — بران تخت 'upon that bed' (بران = 'upon/thereon'; تخت = 'bed/throne'). با مادر 'with (his) mother' (با = 'with'; مادر = 'mother'). افگنده خوار is a compound participial description: افگنده (past participle 'cast/laid') and خوار ('low/humiliated'), so together 'laid low' or 'cast down'. The participle describes what was done to the slain one.
segments.s196-g123: بیک دست بهرام پر آب چشم — بیک دست is an adverbial phrase 'with one hand' (literally 'by one hand'); بهرام is the name (subject). پر آب چشم is a genitive-like adjectival phrase 'eyes full of water' i.e. 'eyes brimming with tears' (پر = 'full (of)'; آب چشم = 'water of the eye' = tears). Word order is topical: instrument/condition (بیک دست) before the name.
segments.s196-g124: نشسته ببالین او پر ز خشم — نشسته is the perfect participle/ adjective 'seated' (here predicative), ببالین 'at the bedside' marks location, او = 'he' (refers back to the slain or to Bahram depending on context), and پر ز خشم is 'full from/of anger' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = poetic 'from/of'; خشم = 'anger'), giving the emotive state 'sitting at the bedside, full of wrath.'
segments.s196-g125: بدست دگر زنگه شاوران — بدست دگر is an adverbial phrase here meaning 'on the other hand' / 'meanwhile' (بدست = 'by/through', دگر = 'other/another'); زنگه شاوران literally 'the ring/circle of advisers' (زنگه used as 'ring/circle' + شاوَران 'advisers'), naming the group whose action follows.
segments.s196-g126: برو انجمن گشته کنداوران — برو 'to that/then' or 'to/into' (here functioning as a motion preposition/verb 'went to'), انجمن = 'assembly/council' is the place, and گشته کنداوران uses گشته (perfect participle/verb 'become/turned' or 'had gone') with کنداوران 'investigators'. Together the clause says the advisers 'turned into' or 'went and acted as investigators' in the council; word order places the place (انجمن) before the resulting state/agent (کنداوران).
segments.s196-g127: گوی چون درختی بران تخت عاج — گوی 'like a sphere/roundness' (used poetically), چون = 'like/as', درختی = 'a tree' (simile), and بران تخت عاج = 'on that ivory throne/bed' (بران = 'upon that'; تخت = 'throne/bed'; عاج = 'ivory'). The whole phrase is a poetic simile comparing the figure's posture or shape to a rounded tree set on an ivory throne.
segments.s196-g128: بدیدار ماه و ببالای ساج — بدیدار here describes appearance/visible aspect ('in appearance' or 'seeming'), ماه = 'moon' (used metaphorically for a beautiful or moon-like face), و = 'and', ببالای = 'on the top of / above' and ساج = the saj tree; together they produce two linked images of appearance: 'moon-faced' and 'with the crest/top like a saj tree.'
segments.s196-g113: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from' placed before the names; وز here is a compressed poetic conjunction (و + ز) linking the two names and meaning 'and from'. The phrase تیز مغز is a two‑word adjective (تیز modifies مغز) meaning 'sharp‑brained' or 'keen of mind' placed after the names to characterize them.
segments.s196-g114: نیاید is a negative present/impersonal verb 'does not come / does not appear' used here to state a general fact. بگیتی is an adverbial phrase 'in the world' (ب + گیتی). یکی directly precedes the noun کار and functions as the numeral 'one / a single'; کار نغز is a nominal phrase 'an excellent/pithy deed' with نغز qualifying کار.
segments.s196-g115: هماننگه is an adverb meaning 'at that same moment / immediately'. بیامد is the past tense 'came'. سپهدار is the title 'spahbed / commander' and طوس is the personal name following the title (Tūs, the hero).
segments.s196-g116: براه کلات is a locative phrase 'on / toward the road to Kalāt' (براه = 'toward/on the road of'). اندر آورد is a verbal cluster where آورد ('brought') is the main verb and اندر marks direction/location; کوس (object) is the war‑drum. Word order is prepositional phrase → verb → object, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s196-g117: چو at the start functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. The pair چو ... و چون ... is a poetic parallelism ('when Goudarz and when/like Giv'), introducing the named commanders. کنداوران is an epithet or collective name attached to them; here it follows the names as a descriptive label.
segments.s196-g118: ز گردان ایران is an ablative/partitive 'from the ranks/troops of Iran' (ز = 'from', گردان = 'troop'). سپاهی گران is a noun phrase 'heavy/weighty soldiers' (گران intensifies سپاهی, meaning here 'mighty' or 'formidable'). The whole segment states origin and character of the fighters.
segments.s196-g119: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed / general'. بسوی سپدکوه شد is a short clause where بسوی marks direction 'toward', سپدکوه is the place name, and شد (here an inchoative/past marker) indicates motion or change of state — 'turned/moved toward Spadkuh'.
segments.s196-g120: وزانجا = 'and from there' (وز = و + ز, poetic). بنزدیکی is an adverb 'nearby / close at hand'. انبوه شد is a verb phrase 'became numerous / the crowd increased' (انبوه = numerous, شد = became). The clause reports that a dense gathering formed near that place.
segments.s196-g105: ببد is an archaic/poetic past form of بودن meaning 'was' and serves as the main past copula here. بس is an intensifier 'so/very' placed before the adjective. درازست is a compound predication (دراز + است) meaning 'is/was long'; in poetry the verb/copula and adjective can be juxtaposed before the nominal subject. چنگ سپهر is a genitive phrase 'the claw/grasp of the heavens' (چنگ = claw/talon, سپهر = the heavens) and functions as the theme whose extent is being described.
segments.s196-g106: به + noun expresses direction/target 'toward the …'. برنگردد is a negative present/future-form verb ('does not/will not turn back' or 'does not turn toward'); here it states that the action of turning does not occur. بیدادگر is the target noun 'the tyrant/oppressor'. بمهر is بم + مهر where بم (= with) marks accompaniment/attitude and مهر here means 'love/mercy' (an adverbial phrase 'with mercy'). Word order (target after به, then verb) is poetic but the whole reads 'does not turn toward the tyrant with mercy.'
segments.s196-g107: زکیخسرو is the addressee (vocative/subject). اکنون is an adverb 'now' placed before the verb for emphasis. ندارید is present 2nd-person plural (or polite singular) of داشتن with negation built into the stem here meaning 'you do not have'; it governs the following nominal object شرم 'shame'. The phrase reads 'Kay Khosrow, you now have no shame.'
segments.s196-g108: که introduces a content/reason clause explaining the absence of shame. چندان before a noun/verb phrase is an intensifier 'so much/to that extent'. سخن گفت here is a fixed collocation meaning 'to speak/talk' where سخن is 'words/speech' and گفت functions as the verbal element ('said/spoke'); in classical/poetic usage گفت can act as the verbal noun or the verbal component in such phrases. با طوس places Tous as the interlocutor ('with Tous'), and نرم (here adverbial) modifies how the speaking was done — 'gently/softly'. Word order (object and manner after the verb) is typical of the poem's style.
segments.s196-g109: بکین is a poetic/archaic verbal form here meaning 'to take vengeance' or 'for vengeance' and functions adverbially to indicate the purpose for which Siyâvash was sent. سیاوش is the proper name and the understood subject of the following clause. فرستادتان is past tense 'you sent' (2nd-person plural/polite) with the enclitic تان marking 'you' as the object; together the phrase reads 'you sent Siyâvash (to take vengeance)'.
segments.s196-g110: بسی is an intensifier here meaning 'much/very many'. پند و اندرزها is a doublet ('advice and counsels') with اندرزها pluralized by -ها to mean 'pieces of counsel/admonitions'. دادتان is past 'gave you' (داد + -تان), so the verb follows the object(s) and yields 'he gave you many counsels.' The whole segment reports that Siyâvash gave them much advice.
segments.s196-g111: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' (here 'concerning' or 'about'), so ز خون برادر = 'about/of the blood of a brother' (i.e., the brother's blood or bloodshed). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; آگه is an adjective/participial adjective meaning 'aware/informed', and شود is the copula/auxiliary 'becomes' (3rd sing.). The clause thus reads 'when one becomes aware of a brother's blood.'
segments.s196-g112: همه 'all' quantifies the following nouns. شرم و آزرم are paired synonyms meaning 'shame and modesty/honor' (آزرم here is an archaic/rare word used alongside شرم). کوته is predicative 'shortened/abridged' (used metaphorically), and شود is the verbal auxiliary 'becomes' (3rd sing.), so the clause literally says 'all shame and modesty become shortened'—understood idiomatically as 'shame and restraint vanish.'
